title: Swollen Cat Leg After Window Trapping: Triage & Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your cat's leg was trapped in a window and is now swollen, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about Window Syndrome and why to seek care.
content_html:
What Is Window Syndrome?

When a cat's limb becomes trapped in a window—particularly tilt-and-turn or sash windows—it often results in a serious condition known as 'Window Syndrome' or crush injury. The pressure from the cat's body weight against the window frame can cause significant damage to the skin, muscles, blood vessels, and nerves. Even if the cat seems fine initially, the internal damage can be progressive and life-threatening.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This situation is considered a High-Urgency Emergency. Swelling is a clear indicator of significant trauma, potentially involving internal bleeding, fluid leakage, or bone fractures. More critically, crush injuries can lead to 'crush syndrome,' where toxins from damaged muscle tissue enter the bloodstream. This can cause sudden kidney failure or heart issues shortly after the pressure is released. You should seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency clinic.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, taking a clear photo of the swollen leg and how your cat is positioning it can be invaluable. A photo allows the veterinary team to visually assess the severity of the swelling and look for signs of vascular compromise (such as blue or pale skin tones) before you even arrive. This helps the hospital prioritize your cat's care and prepare diagnostic equipment, such as X-ray machines or blood gas analyzers, in advance.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if the swelling goes down?
faqs.answer: No. Waiting can lead to irreversible nerve damage or systemic organ failure due to toxins released from the crushed muscle. Immediate intervention is required.

faqs.question: Should I try to splint the leg at home?
faqs.answer: No, do not attempt to splint or bandage the leg. This can cause further pain and potentially cut off what remains of the blood circulation. Keep the cat in a carrier and transport them immediately.

faqs.question: What will the vet do for a window injury?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam, check for neurological reflexes, and use X-rays to check for fractures. They may also start IV fluids to protect the kidneys from muscle-damage toxins.
